mnemonics.title: OS FUNCTIONS
mnemonics.content: OS PROVIDES SERVICES: Execution, I/O, Communication, File handling, Error detection, Protection, Memory management, Process scheduling. REMEMBER: A TIRED FIGHTER NEEDS PROTECTION FROM DANGEROUS MONSTERS

acronyms.usage: Software that manages computer hardware and provides services to applications
Firmware that initializes hardware during computer startup
Language used in early batch systems to describe jobs
Visual interface allowing users to interact with OS through icons and windows
Text-based interface where users type commands to interact with OS
Set of functions applications use to communicate with OS services
Mechanism for processes to communicate with each other

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a sandwich: Bottom layer is HARDWARE (bread), middle is OPERATING SYSTEM (filling), top is USER APPLICATIONS (top bread). The OS sits between hardware and applications, just like filling connects both layers.
Think of boot as a relay race: BIOS (starter) passes baton to BOOTLOADER (first runner), who passes to OS (main runner). Each hands off to next until program execution finishes.
Picture a restaurant kitchen: One chef (CPU) manages multiple dishes (processes). When one dish simmers (waits for I/O), chef works on another, never standing idle.
Imagine system calls as a security gate between city (user mode) and palace (kernel mode). Citizens (applications) must request entry through gatekeepers (system calls) for privileged services.

analogies.analogy: Like a government manages national resources (roads, electricity, defense), the OS manages computer resources (CPU, memory, storage) and provides services to citizens (applications).
A hotel manager (OS) allocates rooms (memory) to guests (processes), manages housekeeping (I/O), handles check-ins/check-outs (program execution), and ensures fair service to all guests.

The kernel is like a power plant providing electricity to an entire city. All homes and businesses (applications) depend on it, but they never interact with the plant directly; they just use the power.
Customers (user programs) cannot enter the kitchen (kernel). They give orders to the waiter (system call), who conveys requests to kitchen and brings back results.
